suburbs.name: Chermside
suburbs.metaTitle: Tree Removal Chermside | Ozzy's Tree & Stump
suburbs.metaDescription: Tree removal, lopping and stump grinding in Chermside, Brisbane Northside. Owner-operated, fully insured, open 7 days. Free quotes. Call 0451 308 349.
suburbs.h1: Tree Removal & Stump Grinding in Chermside
suburbs.heroLine: Local Chermside tree crew, fully insured, free quotes, open 7 days.
suburbs.introHtml:
Chermside is our home base, so a job here usually means we are a few streets away rather than driving in from the other side of Brisbane. We work right across the suburb, from the post-war homes on the old War Service blocks near Sparkes Paddock to the newer townhouses and unit complexes that have gone up around Westfield and Gympie Road.

The older parts of Chermside have had decades for trees to get big. A lot of the back yards on the 1960s blocks now carry mature gums, leopard trees and the odd poinciana that has outgrown the space it was planted in. We handle the lot here: tree removal, tree lopping and pruning, stump grinding, palm work, and storm clean-ups when the Northside cops a summer thunderstorm.

We are owner-operated, fully insured, and rated 5.0 across 27 Google reviews. Phone Ozzy on 0451 308 349 for a free quote.
suburbs.localContextHtml:
Most Chermside yards we get called to have the usual Northside mix. Tall spotted gums and other eucalypts that drop limbs and litter gutters, the occasional large fig or leopard tree near the front fence, and plenty of palms, mostly cocos and queen palms, that have got too tall to clean from a ladder. Out towards Chermside Hills and the Raven Street Reserve end of the suburb the bushland is open eucalypt forest, so homes backing onto it often want gums thinned, deadwood taken out, or trees cleared back off the roof and the fence line.

Block sizes vary a fair bit, which changes how we approach a job. On the tighter newer subdivisions and the unit sites we will often bring the cherry picker so we can take a tree down in sections without dropping anything on a shed, a pool or the neighbour's fence. The mini track loader and the tracked stump grinder fit through a standard side gate, so we can grind stumps out the back without tearing up the lawn getting there.
suburbs.whyUsHtml: Being based in Chermside is the practical part. We know the streets, we know how tight some of the older driveways and the unit complexes get, and we can usually get to you quickly for a quote or after a storm. When a gum comes down across a Chermside driveway at night, you want a crew that is local and can get the gear there, not one stuck in traffic on Gympie Road from somewhere else. We bring our own cherry picker, stump grinder, loader and chipper, so most jobs get done in one visit and the site is left clean.

faqs.q: Do you do emergency tree work in Chermside after storms?
faqs.a: Yes. We are based in Chermside and open 7 days, so after a summer storm we can usually get to local jobs quickly. If a tree or large limb has come down on a house, fence or driveway, call Ozzy on 0451 308 349 and we will sort out making it safe and clearing it.

faqs.q: Can you grind out a stump in a small Chermside back yard?
faqs.a: Yes. Our tracked stump grinder fits through a standard side gate, so we can reach stumps in the back yards on the tighter newer blocks and the older post-war homes without taking the fence down. We grind below the surface and clean up the grindings so you can turf or replant over the top.

suburbs.name: Stafford
suburbs.metaTitle: Tree Removal Stafford | Ozzy's Tree & Stump
suburbs.metaDescription: Tree removal, lopping, pruning and stump grinding in Stafford. Local Chermside crew, fully insured, open 7 days, free quotes. Call 0451 308 349.
suburbs.h1: Tree Removal and Stump Grinding in Stafford
suburbs.heroLine: A local Northside crew handling Stafford's big backyard trees and tight access blocks, every day of the week.
suburbs.introHtml:
Stafford is one of those older Northside suburbs where the trees have had decades to get big. A lot of the homes are post-war timber places and Queenslanders on quarter-acre blocks, and the backyards came with mature gums, mango trees, poincianas and the odd jacaranda that have grown well past the back fence. We work in Stafford most weeks, so we usually know what is behind those houses before we turn up.

We are based just up the road in Chermside, roughly four kilometres away, so getting a crew and gear to a Stafford job is quick. That matters when a limb has come down across a driveway after a storm, or when you just want someone to show up and give you a straight answer on a tree that has you worried.

The work we do here covers tree removal, tree lopping and pruning, stump grinding, palm removal and cleaning, and storm and emergency call-outs. We are fully insured, we quote for free, and we are open seven days.
suburbs.localContextHtml:
The big jobs in Stafford tend to be backyard trees that have outgrown the block. Forest red gums and other eucalypts are common around here, and they will drop large limbs without much warning, so they need a proper look from someone who knows what they are doing. Poincianas throw out heavy horizontal limbs that catch the wind in a summer storm, mangoes get dense and shady right over the house, and a lot of yards have a row of palms that need the dead fronds and seed pods cleared out each year.

Access is the part that catches people out in Stafford. The streets are tight and a lot of these blocks have a narrow side gate and a fence line close to the neighbour, so you cannot always swing a big machine straight in. We run a mini track loader (an Avant) that fits through standard gates, a tracked stump grinder for the same reason, and a cherry picker for the tall removals near the house or close to Kedron Brook. Keep in mind that bigger gums, eucalypts and pines can be protected by Brisbane City Council, so it is worth checking whether a permit is needed before anything comes down.
suburbs.whyUsHtml: Being based in Chermside means we are only a few minutes from Stafford, so call-outs are fast and we are not adding travel onto your quote from the other side of town. We know the suburb's older blocks and the access problems that come with them, we clean up properly before we leave, and if a tree comes down across your yard in a storm we can usually be there the same day. It is owner-operated, so the person who quotes the job is the person doing the work.

faqs.q: Do you cover all of Stafford, including the tight blocks near Kedron Brook?
faqs.a: Yes. We work right across Stafford, from the streets up near Stafford City and the Sparkes Hill reservoir down to the blocks backing onto Kedron Brook. The narrow side access on the older post-war homes is normal for us, which is why we run a mini track loader and a tracked stump grinder that fit through a standard gate.

faqs.q: Can you grind out a stump after the tree is gone?
faqs.a: We can. Stump grinding is one of the main things we get asked for in Stafford, often on old gum or mango stumps left from a removal years ago. The tracked grinder gets into backyards without tearing up the lawn, and we grind below the surface so you can turf or plant over it. We can take the mulch away or leave it for the garden, your call.

suburbs.name: Aspley
suburbs.metaTitle: Tree Removal Aspley | Ozzy's Tree & Stump
suburbs.metaDescription: Tree removal, lopping, pruning and stump grinding in Aspley. Chermside-based crew, fully insured, open 7 days, free quotes. Call 0451 308 349.
suburbs.h1: Tree Removal and Stump Grinding in Aspley
suburbs.heroLine: Local Chermside crew working Aspley's big blocks and mature gums, seven days a week.
suburbs.introHtml:
Aspley sits about 12km north of the city along the Gympie Road corridor, and it is one of the better Northside suburbs for big, established trees. A lot of the blocks came out of the 1960s subdivisions of the old market gardens and farms, so they tend to be larger than the newer estates, with room for mature gums that have had decades to put on height and spread.

That size is good to live with and a bit of a handful to maintain. We are based just down the road in Chermside, roughly a five minute drive from most of Aspley, so getting a crew and gear to your place is quick and the quote costs you nothing.

We handle tree removal, tree lopping and pruning, stump grinding, palm work and storm clean-up across the whole suburb, from the streets running off Maundrell Terrace and Robinson Road through to the homes backing onto Little Cabbage Tree Creek.
suburbs.localContextHtml:
The gum trees do most of the heavy lifting in Aspley's tree cover. Forest red gum and spotted gum are common on the clay soils through here, and once they mature they get tall, heavy-limbed and prone to dropping branches in a Brisbane storm. We prune to Australian Standard AS 4373 so the tree keeps its shape and health rather than getting hacked back. Worth knowing too: Brisbane City Council protects a lot of these trees, and many established trees need a permit before they can be removed, so it pays to get the tree assessed before any work starts.

The other regulars are tall palms, often self-seeded and overgrown, that need cleaning out or taking down, plus the usual mix of established plantings on the bigger garden blocks. Whatever is on your property, we bring the right kit, including a cherry picker for height, an Avant mini loader for tight access, a tracked stump grinder and a wood chipper to deal with the timber on site.
suburbs.whyUsHtml: Being based in Chermside means Aspley is basically our backyard. We know the streets, the access on the larger blocks and the species we are likely to find before we even pull up. That local knowledge keeps quotes accurate and jobs moving, and it means we can get to you fast when a limb comes down after a storm. We are owner-operated and fully insured, rated 5.0 across 27 Google reviews, and you deal with the bloke doing the work, not a call centre.

faqs.q: Do I need a council permit to remove a gum tree in Aspley?
faqs.a: Often, yes. Brisbane City Council protects a lot of vegetation, and many established trees need a permit before removal. We can look at the tree, tell you where it stands and point you in the right direction before any work happens. Pruning to keep a tree healthy is usually more straightforward than full removal.

faqs.q: How quickly can you get to Aspley for storm or emergency work?
faqs.a: Quickly. We work out of Chermside, only a few minutes from most of Aspley, so for fallen limbs or storm damage we can usually get a crew out fast. Call 0451 308 349 and we will sort out what needs doing first to make the place safe.

suburbs.name: Kedron
suburbs.metaTitle: Tree Removal Kedron | Ozzy's Tree & Stump
suburbs.metaDescription: Tree removal, lopping and stump grinding in Kedron 4031. Local Chermside crew, fully insured, free quotes, open 7 days. Call 0451 308 349.
suburbs.h1: Tree Removal and Stump Grinding in Kedron
suburbs.heroLine: Local Chermside crew doing tree work across Kedron, from the Avenues down to the Brook.
suburbs.introHtml:
Kedron sits about 7km north of the city, wrapped around Gympie Road and the Brook that gives the suburb its name. It is an established area and the trees show it. The character Queenslanders through the Avenues east of Leckie Road tend to have big old gums and leafy backyards that have had decades to grow, and a lot of that timber now sits close to houses, fences and power lines.

We are based just up the road in Chermside, so Kedron is a few minutes away. We can get out for a quote quickly and we know the local conditions, including the streets down near the Kedron Brook floodplain that cop it during a heavy summer storm.

We do the lot: tree removal, tree lopping and pruning, stump grinding, palm work, storm damage and land clearing. Owner-operated and fully insured, and we will give you a straight answer on what a tree actually needs.
suburbs.localContextHtml:
The mature street and backyard trees through Kedron are a mix of large eucalypts (forest red gum and the like), jacarandas and poincianas, plus the cocos and other palms that came with a lot of the older homes. Big gums drop limbs in a southerly change, jacarandas get brittle with age, and self-seeded palms tend to crowd back fences. Each one needs a different approach, and we prune to Australian Standard AS 4373 so a tree that is staying is left in good shape rather than just topped.

The low-lying pockets near Kedron Brook are worth keeping an eye on. Waterlogged ground after the wet season can loosen the root plate on bigger trees, and a leaning gum over a house is the kind of job we would rather look at sooner than later. If a storm has already brought something down, we run a cherry picker, a tracked stump grinder, a wood chipper and an Avant mini loader, so we can get into tight Kedron blocks and clean up properly.
suburbs.whyUsHtml: Being a Chermside crew, Kedron is basically our backyard. We can usually swing past for a free quote the same week, we know the narrow driveways and tight side accesses on the older blocks, and we turn up when we say we will. We are open 7 days and rated 5.0 on Google. Give us a call on 0451 308 349 and we will sort it out.

faqs.q: Do you do tree work in the flood-prone parts of Kedron near the Brook?
faqs.a: Yes. The low-lying streets along the Kedron Brook floodplain are some of the spots we get called to most, especially after heavy rain when the ground gets waterlogged and bigger trees start to lean. We will come and assess it and tell you honestly whether it needs removing or can be safely pruned.

faqs.q: Can you get machinery into the older Kedron blocks with tight access?
faqs.a: Usually, yes. A lot of the homes through the Avenues and the older streets have narrow side access, so we run an Avant mini loader and a tracked stump grinder that fit through standard gateways. If access is genuinely too tight we will work it by hand. We will confirm what is possible when we quote.

articles.metaTitle: Tree Lopping vs Pruning: The Difference
articles.metaDescription: The real difference between tree lopping and proper pruning to AS 4373, why topping harms trees, and what to ask a Brisbane arborist before any work.
articles.h1: Tree lopping versus pruning: what's the difference and why it matters
articles.dek: Most people in Brisbane search for "tree lopping" when what they really want is a properly pruned tree. Here's the difference, and why the cuts you make today decide what your tree does for the next ten years.
articles.bodyHtml:
Drive around the Northside after a rough summer and you'll spot it. A big old gum or poinciana that's had its whole top sawn off flat, leaving a row of fat stubs. Six months later that same tree is covered in a thick brush of skinny new shoots, all sprouting from the cut ends. It looks busy and green, so people assume it worked. It didn't. That's lopping, and the tree is now in worse shape than before anyone touched it.

Here's the thing though. Half the people who ring us asking for "tree lopping" don't actually want their tree topped. They want it made smaller, or safer, or pulled back off the roof. "Lopping" is just the word most of us grew up using. So this isn't about correcting anyone. It's about explaining what the cuts actually do, so you know what you're paying for.

What lopping actually is

Lopping, or topping, means cutting branches back to a stub with no thought for where the next growth will come from. The saw goes through the main stems at whatever height suits, often halfway along a branch where there's no side limb to take over. It's quick and it's cheap, which is exactly why some operators still do it.

The problem is what happens next. A topped tree responds by panicking. It throws out a dense cluster of fast shoots, called water sprouts or epicormic growth, right below each cut. Those shoots only attach to the outer layers of the old wood, so they're weakly joined. Within a few years they're heavy, and the first decent storm snaps them clean off. The tree ends up taller and more dangerous than it ever was.

On top of that, a big flat cut through a main stem leaves a wound the tree struggles to seal. Rot, fungal decay and borers get in through the open face and work their way down into the trunk. You often can't see it from the ground. The tree looks alive and leafy while it quietly hollows out.

What proper pruning looks like

Good pruning follows the Australian Standard AS 4373, Pruning of Amenity Trees. It's the rulebook qualified arborists work to. The core idea is simple. Make the smallest cuts needed to do the job, cut in the right place so the wound can seal, and never take more of the canopy than the tree can carry.

The standard covers a handful of jobs, each with a proper name and a proper method:

  • Crown thinning. Selectively taking out some inner branches to let wind and light pass through, without changing the tree's overall shape or size. Worth doing before storm season because it lowers wind resistance.
  • Crown lifting. Removing the lowest branches to give clearance over a driveway, fence or footpath.
  • Crown reduction. Making the canopy smaller by cutting each branch back to a healthy side limb that takes over as the new growing tip. Done right, the tree keeps its natural shape, just smaller.
  • Deadwooding. Taking out the dead and dying branches that drop without warning.

The difference between crown reduction and topping is the whole point. Reduction cuts go back to a living branch big enough to keep growing in that direction. The tree heals neatly and the regrowth is strong. Topping cuts go to a stub with nothing below them, and you get the weak sprouts and the rot. A good arborist generally won't take more than about a quarter of the live canopy in one go, because past that you're robbing the tree of the leaves it needs to feed itself.

When reduction or thinning is the right call

Plenty of Brisbane trees genuinely need work. A jacaranda hanging over the roofline, a leopard tree shedding limbs over the kids' trampoline, a camphor laurel blocking every bit of light from the back deck. Crown reduction brings the size down properly. Thinning opens it up so a gale blows through it rather than against it. If a tree is structurally sound, these are far better outcomes than removal, and they last.

Timing matters here too. Brisbane's storm season runs roughly November through March, and our worst call-outs land right in the middle of it. The sensible window to prepare is late winter into early spring, say July to September, so deadwood and weak limbs are gone before the first southerly buster rolls in.

A quick word on council rules

Before any major work, it's worth knowing your tree might be protected. Brisbane City Council protects a lot of vegetation under its Natural Assets Local Law and the City Plan 2014 overlays, and that can apply even to a tree in your own backyard. You can check your address on Council's online property search under the vegetation layer. Permits are free, but the fines for clearing a protected tree without one are not. Dead trees and genuine immediate hazards are usually exempt, though you'll often need photos or an arborist's report to back it up. We sort this out as part of the job.

What to ask a tree company

You don't need to be an expert. A few straight questions tell you most of what you need to know:

  1. Do you prune to AS 4373?
  2. Are you fully insured, and can I see the certificate?
  3. Where will you make the cuts, and roughly how much of the canopy are you taking?
  4. Is my tree protected, and will you handle the council side?

If someone offers to "top it right back" for a suspiciously low price and can't answer those, that's your answer. The cheap cut today is the expensive, dangerous tree in five years.

If you've got a tree you're not sure about, give Ozzy's a call on 0451 308 349. Happy to take a look, give you honest advice and a free quote, no pressure either way.

faqs.q: Is tree lopping illegal in Brisbane?
faqs.a: Lopping itself isn't outlawed, but it's poor practice that damages trees, and a qualified arborist will prune to AS 4373 instead. Separately, many trees in Brisbane are protected under Council's Natural Assets Local Law or City Plan overlays, so the work may need a free Council permit regardless of how it's done. You can check your address on Council's online property search under the vegetation layer.

faqs.q: Will crown reduction hurt my tree?
faqs.a: Not when it's done properly. Crown reduction cuts each branch back to a healthy side limb that takes over the growth, so the tree heals neatly and keeps its shape. The key is not removing too much at once, generally no more than about a quarter of the live canopy in a session. That's the difference between reduction and topping, which removes far more and leaves weak, decay-prone stubs.

faqs.q: When should I prune my trees before storm season?
faqs.a: Brisbane's storm season runs roughly November to March, so the best time to prepare is late winter into early spring, around July to September. That gives you time to clear deadwood, remove weak or overextended limbs, and thin the crown so wind passes through rather than pushing against a dense canopy. Leaving it until a storm warning is on the radio is leaving it too late.

articles.metaTitle: Council Approval to Remove a Tree in Brisbane?
articles.metaDescription: A plain guide to Brisbane City Council tree rules: when you need a permit, the dead and dangerous exemption, how to check your block, and the fines.
articles.h1: Do you need council approval to remove a tree in Brisbane?
articles.dek: Some Brisbane trees you can drop tomorrow. Others will cost you thousands if you touch them without a permit. Here is how to tell the difference before the chainsaw comes out.
articles.bodyHtml:
We get this call most weeks. Someone has a gum leaning over the back patio, or a camphor laurel dropping limbs on the kids' trampoline, and they want it gone. Fair enough. The first question we ask is not about the tree. It is about the block. In Brisbane, whether you can legally remove a tree has very little to do with how annoying it is, and a lot to do with where it stands and what protections sit over your land.

Here is the honest version, in plain terms. This is general information to help you ask the right questions. It is not legal advice, and every property is different, so check your own block before anyone starts cutting.

The two sets of rules that matter

Brisbane City Council controls vegetation through two main instruments, and a tree can be caught by either.

The first is the Natural Assets Local Law 2003, usually shortened to NALL. This protects "protected vegetation" across the city. That covers trees in bushland, wetlands and waterway corridors, plus significant native and significant urban vegetation on private land. Council can also place a Vegetation Protection Order (VPO) over a specific tree or stand of trees on private property. If a VPO applies, that tree is protected and you need permission before you work on it.

The second is City Plan 2014, Council's planning scheme. It carries overlays such as the biodiversity areas overlay and the significant landscape tree overlay. If your property falls inside one of these, removing or even heavily pruning a tree can trigger an approval requirement on top of the local law.

So a tree might be protected because of what it is, because of a VPO, or because of an overlay sitting over your land. Sometimes more than one applies at once.

When you probably do NOT need a permit

Plenty of ordinary backyard trees are not protected, and you can deal with them without involving Council. As a rough guide, a tree is more likely to be fair game if:

  • It sits on a standard residential block with no VPO and no relevant City Plan overlay.
  • It is a common garden or exotic species, not significant native or significant urban vegetation.
  • It is not on Council land, a verge, or a neighbour's property.

The catch is that "probably" is doing real work in that sentence. The only way to know is to check, which we get to below.

When you DO need approval

You need a permit before you remove or work on a tree if it is protected under the NALL, if it is covered by a Vegetation Protection Order, or if it sits inside a City Plan overlay that regulates vegetation. Mature natives, trees in or near waterway corridors, and anything flagged as a significant landscape tree are common triggers on Northside blocks around Chermside, Aspley, Stafford and out toward the bushier suburbs.

The permits themselves are free to apply for. Council offers short-term permits for one-off jobs like removing or pruning a tree, longer-term permits for ongoing maintenance, and separate permission for minor work on Council street trees. Free does not mean automatic, though. Council assesses the application, and it can say no.

How to check your own property

Do not guess, and do not take a neighbour's word for it. Two reliable ways to check:

  1. Use Council's City Plan online mapping tool on the Brisbane City Council website. Enter your address and look at the overlays, including the biodiversity and significant landscape tree layers, plus any Vegetation Protection Order on the property.
  2. Phone Council directly on 07 3403 8888 and ask them to confirm what applies to your address. They deal with this all day.

Five minutes on the mapping tool can save you a fine with five figures in it.

The dead and dangerous tree exemption

There is a narrow exemption for trees that are genuinely dead, or that pose an immediate and serious threat to people or property. This is the one people lean on after a storm. It is real, but it is tighter than most expect.

"Immediate and significant threat" means exactly that. A tree you do not like the look of, or one that drops leaves in your gutters, does not qualify. And the burden of proof sits with you. Council can ask you to show the tree met the exemption, and for a protected or significant tree that often means photos of the structural problem and, in some cases, a report from an AQF Level 5 arborist. Take the photos and keep them before the tree comes down, not after, because once it is mulched there is nothing left to assess.

Storm season here runs roughly November to March, and that is when the genuinely dangerous calls spike. Even then, if there is any doubt about whether a tree is protected, a quick call to Council first is cheaper than the alternative.

The penalties are not small

Removing or damaging protected vegetation without permission is an offence, and Council enforces it. Penalties range from on-the-spot infringement notices through to prosecution, and serious cases reach into the tens of thousands of dollars. On top of the fine, Council can order replacement planting and restoration. It is not worth the gamble on a tree you could have had assessed for free.

Not sure? Ask before you cut

If you are unsure whether your tree is protected, check the mapping tool or ring Council first. And if you would rather have someone look at it in person, give Ozzy's a call on 0451 308 349 for a free quote or a straight answer. We work across Brisbane's Northside, we know which trees raise questions, and we would always rather sort the paperwork than see a customer cop a fine.

faqs.q: Can I remove a tree on my own property in Brisbane without telling Council?
faqs.a: Sometimes, yes. If the tree is an ordinary garden species on a standard block with no Vegetation Protection Order and no relevant City Plan 2014 overlay, you generally do not need a permit. But if it is protected under the Natural Assets Local Law, covered by a VPO, or sits inside a vegetation overlay, you need approval first. Check your address on Council's City Plan online mapping tool or phone 07 3403 8888 before you start.

faqs.q: What if a storm makes a tree dangerous overnight?
faqs.a: There is an exemption for trees that are dead or pose an immediate and significant threat to people or property, which covers genuine storm emergencies. The threat has to be real and immediate, not just inconvenient. Take clear photos of the damage before the tree is removed, because Council can ask you to prove the tree met the exemption, and for significant trees that may mean an arborist report.

faqs.q: How much is the fine for removing a protected tree without a permit?
faqs.a: It varies with the severity of the offence. Penalties run from infringement notices up to prosecution, and serious cases can reach tens of thousands of dollars. Council can also order replacement planting and restoration on top of any fine. Given the permits are free to apply for, it is not a sensible risk to take.

articles.metaTitle: Get Your Trees Ready for Brisbane Storm Season
articles.metaDescription: A practical guide for Northside Brisbane homeowners on prepping trees before storm season, spotting hazards, and what to do after storm damage.
articles.h1: How to get your trees ready for Brisbane storm season
articles.dek: A practical, Northside Brisbane guide to checking your trees before the storms hit, cutting the risk of damage, and handling a damaged tree safely once the weather has done its worst.
articles.bodyHtml:
The first big southerly of the season rolls through about 4pm, the sky goes that bruised green colour, and within twenty minutes a limb is on the roof and the power is out. We see it every summer across the Northside. The frustrating part is how much of that damage was avoidable. A tree that drops a branch in a storm has usually been telling you for months that the branch was dodgy. Most people just never looked up.

South East Queensland cops more storms than almost anywhere in the country, and the season runs roughly November through to March, with the odd early one in October. The work you want done is the work you do before the clouds build, not during. Here is what to check and when to get someone in.

Walk your yard and actually look up

Pick a calm afternoon, stand back from each tree, and look at the whole canopy. You are not after perfection, you are after the things that fail under load. Here is what we look for first.

  • Deadwood. Bare grey branches with no leaves, especially up high. Dead limbs are brittle and they are the first thing to come down in wind. A gum can hold dead wood for a long time before it lets go.
  • Overhanging and rubbing limbs. Anything sitting over the roof, the carport, the pool, or the neighbour's fence. Branches that cross and rub each other wear a wound open over time, and that weak point is where they snap.
  • Cracks and weak V-forks. Where two stems grow up in a tight V with bark squeezed between them, that join is weak. Under storm load it can split right down the trunk. A visible crack at a fork is a serious warning.
  • Leaning trees. A tree that has always leaned the same way is usually fine. A new lean, or fresh cracked soil and lifting roots on one side, means the root plate is moving. That one needs a look sooner rather than later.
  • Lifting roots and soft ground. Roots heaving up out of the lawn, or boggy ground around the base after rain, both reduce how well the tree is anchored.
  • Vines. Heavy vine growth adds weight and acts like a sail in the wind. It also hides what is going on with the branches underneath.

Palms get their own check. Old fronds and heavy seed pods come down hard and fast in wind, and a tall palm hanging over an entertaining area is worth cleaning up before summer.

Why pruning and deadwooding before the season works

A storm does not test your whole tree evenly. It finds the weakest part and pulls on it. Take the dead and damaged wood out beforehand and you have removed most of the easy failures. Thinning the canopy a little also lets wind pass through instead of pushing against a solid wall of leaves, which lowers the load on the trunk and the roots.

Good pruning is not topping a tree or hacking it back to stumps. That just triggers a mess of weak regrowth that is more dangerous in a year or two. Proper work follows the Australian Standard for pruning amenity trees, AS 4373, which means clean cuts in the right spots that the tree can actually seal over. Done right, the tree is safer and still looks like a tree.

Check the council rules before you cut

This catches people out. In Brisbane, some trees are protected under the Council's Natural Assets Local Law 2003, and you can need a permit to prune or remove them even when they are on your own property. There are also Vegetation Protection Orders on specific trees. Cutting a protected tree without permission can bring a fine.

The good news is the permits are free to apply for. If you are not sure whether a tree is protected, you can check with Brisbane City Council on 07 3403 8888 before any work starts. A decent tree company will know the local rules and can point you the right way.

What to do with a damaged tree after a storm

Once the weather has passed and it is safe to go outside, have a careful look. Some damage is cosmetic and the tree will be fine. Some of it is not, and the danger does not always end when the wind stops.

Watch for hung-up branches caught in the canopy, partly split limbs still attached, and a trunk that has cracked or shifted. A branch hanging by a strip of bark can drop hours later with no warning. Keep people and cars out from underneath until it is dealt with.

The one rule that matters most

Never go near fallen powerlines. Not to move a branch, not to take a photo, not for anything. Treat every downed line as live, because it very well might be, and lines can hide under fallen leaves and timber. Stay back at least 10 metres, keep kids and pets away, and call Energex on 13 19 62, or 000 if anyone is hurt or there is immediate danger. A tree is replaceable. Wait for someone trained to deal with it.

When to get a professional in

Plenty of light tidy-up work is fine to do yourself from the ground. The line we draw is height, weight, and powerlines. Call someone in when you are looking at any of these.

  • Work that needs a ladder, a chainsaw above shoulder height, or climbing into the tree.
  • Anything over or near powerlines, the house, or a shared fence.
  • A large limb under tension, hung up in the canopy, or partly split and still attached.
  • A leaning tree, lifting roots, or a cracked trunk.
  • Storm cleanup with big sections of trunk to cut up and shift.

For the bigger jobs we bring a cherry picker for height, a tracked stump grinder, a mini loader and a chipper, so the tree comes down in a controlled way and the mess goes with us.

If you want a hand working out what needs doing before the season, give Ozzy's a call on 0451 308 349 for a free quote or just some honest advice. Happy to take a look.

faqs.q: When is storm season in Brisbane and when should I prune my trees?
faqs.a: South East Queensland storm season runs roughly from November to March, sometimes starting in October. The best time to prune and deadwood is before it begins, so spring or early in the season while the weather is still settled. That way the weak and dead branches are gone before the first big winds arrive.

faqs.q: Do I need council approval to remove or prune a tree on my own property in Brisbane?
faqs.a: Sometimes, yes. Some trees in Brisbane are protected under the Council's Natural Assets Local Law 2003, and others have a Vegetation Protection Order on them, which can mean you need a permit even on your own land. The permits are free to apply for. If you are unsure, check with Brisbane City Council on 07 3403 8888 before any work starts.

faqs.q: What should I do if a tree brings down a powerline in a storm?
faqs.a: Stay well clear and treat the line as live, because it may be, and lines can hide under fallen branches. Keep back at least 10 metres, keep children and pets away, and call Energex on 13 19 62. Call 000 if anyone is injured or there is immediate danger. Never try to move the branch or the line yourself.
